feed.title: Lukas Strobel
feed.subtitle: PhD Researcher at HCI & Accessibility (KIT Karlsruhe)

entry.title: Best Paper Award @ MUM ‘25
entry.content: We are we glad to receive this symbol of appreciation for our research “Exploring The Role of Technology for Accessible Stadium Experiences for People With Limited Mobility” from the MUM ‘25 committee. Congratulations to all authors! You can read the paper in the ACM DL.
entry.summary: We are we glad to receive this symbol of appreciation for our research “Exploring The Role of Technology for Accessible Stadium Experiences for People With Limited Mobility” from the MUM ‘25 committee. Congratulations to all authors! You can read the paper in the ACM DL.

entry.title: ASSETS Paper Accepted
entry.content:
How can AI help making lecture videos accessible for student who are blind or have low vision?

In Katharina Anderer’s paper, we investigated this questions and interviewed these students to develop a prototype that can help them navigate lecture material with a AI-powered chatbot.

You can read the paper here.
entry.summary: How can AI help making lecture videos accessible for student who are blind or have low vision?

entry.title: FDG Paper Accepted
entry.content:
How is disengagement approached in competitive eSports games, and which aspects of gameplay affect players’ experiences of disengagement?

We invistigated this question using an interview-based approach: Our results highlight the various factors that influence how eSports teams experience disengagement (see figure below). You can read all details here here.
entry.summary: How is disengagement approached in competitive eSports games, and which aspects of gameplay affect players’ experiences of disengagement?

entry.title: CHI Paper Accepted
entry.content:
How do interactive seat plans for public events reflect accessibility information?

We invistigated this question using first-person methods: The first author explored their interaction with these seat plans and reported on their experiences. This deep introspection showcased that buying tickets as a mobility-disabled person can be complicated as it is difficult to find accessible seats on these representations. However, the results also show that improved ticket purchasing systems bear potential to ease this process, thereby also empowering disabled people and decreasing dependency on other people.

You can read more about this unique personal account here.
entry.summary: How do interactive seat plans for public events reflect accessibility information?

entry.title: TOCHI Article Accepted
entry.content:
How does HCI accessibility research approach the design of sports technology for disabled users?

We explored this question in a literature review that has just been accepted to ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction (TOCHI), showing that sports technology for disabled people often prioritizes health over leisure (although this doesn’t need to be a dichotomy, of course), and that value frameworks for the design of sports technology need to be expanded to reflect perspectives of disabled persons.

You can read our full paper here , and we hope that it’ll inspire future research on sports technology and the role of leisure and joy for all of us.
entry.summary: How does HCI accessibility research approach the design of sports technology for disabled users?
